subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree, completed in their late 60s.
Graduate studies focused on finance, accounting, or a closely related field.
Participated in training sessions on ERP and accounting software as part of ongoing education.
Completed professional development courses in financial reporting and compliance.
Attended seminars on AI in finance during Master’s program.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Current job title: Accountant in the finance department at Osage.
Responsible for managing financial reporting, budgeting, and compliance tasks.
Prepares financial statements and analyzes financial spreadsheets.
Handles data reconciliation and reporting using accounting software.
Frequently collaborates with colleagues on compliance and financial analysis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the finance industry, specifically in financial accounting and reporting.
Sector focus includes finance law, architecture of financial services, and investments.
Role centers on compliance and financial management within a corporate setting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at Osage, a mid-sized organization with a structured finance department.
The finance team consists of multiple accountants, analysts, and compliance officers.
Work environment is collaborative but process-driven, with an emphasis on accuracy and compliance.
Company utilizes a mix of traditional and AI-powered financial tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Osage operates in the financial services sector, providing financial management and reporting solutions.
Market segment includes corporate finance, compliance, and financial analysis services.
Clients and stakeholders include internal business units and external auditors.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as an accountant at Osage.
Transitioned into this finance role after completing advanced education.
Relatively new to the accounting field, but brings strong academic background.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Currently serves at an entry to mid-level professional capacity.
Not in a managerial or executive position, but handles core responsibilities independently.
Provides input for process improvements, especially regarding automation and AI.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Prepares and reviews financial statements in accordance with compliance standards.
Conducts budgeting and forecasting through data analysis and spreadsheet management.
Performs data reconciliation and adjustment using ERP and accounting software.
Analyzes revenue and expense data to support decision-making.
Participates in compliance audits and prepares necessary documentation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends roughly 40% of time on financial reporting and statement preparation.
Budgeting and forecasting occupy about 25% of daily workload.
Data reconciliation and adjustment take up approximately 20% of time.
The remaining 15% is split between compliance tasks and ad-hoc analysis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Works independently on routine financial reporting and analysis tasks.
Makes recommendations for adjustments based on data findings but requires managerial approval for major changes.
Has autonomy to manage daily workflow and prioritize tasks.
Collaborates with senior colleagues on compliance and high-stakes reporting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Data entry and spreadsheet analysis using Microsoft Excel are performed with high confidence.
Generating routine monthly financial statements is straightforward due to established templates.
Reconciling accounts using ERP system is mastered through repetition.
Using QuickBooks for transaction tracking is considered simple and intuitive.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Interpreting new compliance regulations can require extensive research.
Handling complex audits with multiple data sources is demanding.
Ensuring accuracy in large-scale data reconciliation across systems is challenging.
Forecasting scenarios that involve unusual or volatile revenue streams feels complex.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Routine data reconciliation across accounts is highly repetitive.
Compiling monthly financial report data from multiple sources is data-heavy and time-consuming.
Generating standardized compliance documentation is suitable for automation.
Flagging transaction anomalies could be streamlined with AI.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys financial analysis that provides actionable insights for the business.
Finds satisfaction in solving reconciliation discrepancies.
Prefers interactive forecasting over repetitive data entry.
Tedious tasks include manual data consolidation and preparing standard compliance forms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry increases the risk of errors and slows down processes.
Switching between incompatible systems creates workflow bottlenecks.
Lack of real-time data integration between ERP and accounting tools delays reconciliation.
Duplicate data validation across platforms adds unnecessary steps.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Tasks where technical proficiency is high tend to be more enjoyable.
Higher productivity is observed during financial analysis, which is both engaging and skill-aligned.
Monotonous, low-skill tasks are less enjoyable and may reduce overall motivation.
Learning new tools for complex tasks can be frustrating initially, but satisfaction increases with mastery.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open to adopting new technologies that enhance workflow.
Sees value in learning tools that automate repetitive tasks.
Approaches new software with cautious optimism.
Motivated by the tangible benefits of automation and AI in daily work.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts to new tools with moderate speed, leveraging prior experience in Excel and ERP systems.
Requires hands-on practice to become fully comfortable with unfamiliar interfaces.
Finds detailed tutorials and peer support helpful in mastering new systems.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time each month to learn updates in accounting or AI tools.
Prefers practical, task-focused training sessions over theoretical ones.
Balancing training with workload can be a challenge, but sees it as necessary for staying current.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for increased efficiency and accuracy.
High current workload can limit time available for training.
Perceived relevance to core responsibilities is a key factor in prioritizing which tools to learn.
Unclear organizational support for training can be a barrier.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers sharing positive experiences with new tools increases willingness to try them.
Management endorsement is crucial for adoption of AI tools.
Collaborative learning sessions with colleagues boost confidence in using new technologies.
A culture of innovation within the department encourages experimentation with automation.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses an ERP system daily for financial data management and reporting.
Relies on Microsoft Excel for data analysis, spreadsheet creation, and visualization.
QuickBooks is used for transaction tracking and accounting entries.
AI-powered financial tools, such as Kiva, assist with forecasting and compliance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Microsoft Excel is essential for ad-hoc data analysis.
QuickBooks supports day-to-day accounting operations.
Email and shared drives are used for document management and collaboration.
Traditional calculators and printed reference materials are occasionally used for quick checks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with major accounting platforms like QuickBooks and Excel.
Comfortable navigating ERP systems commonly used in finance departments.
Has basic exposure to compliance auditing software.
Familiar with AI-enhanced financial analysis tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally experiences data transfer issues between ERP and Excel.
Manual export/import is sometimes needed due to lack of seamless integration.
AI tools like Kiva require additional setup to synchronize with legacy accounting systems.
Duplicate data entry can occur when systems are not fully integrated.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI concepts such as machine learning, automation, and data modeling.
Familiar with the role of AI in anomaly detection and forecasting.
Can explain the difference between rule-based automation and learning-based AI.
Has read introductory articles on LLMs and AI in finance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses Kiva, an AI-powered financial tool, for forecasting and compliance.
Employs compliance auditing software with embedded AI features.
Occasionally uses LLM-based assistants for drafting reports or analyzing financial data.
Tests new AI modules within the ERP system as they are rolled out.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Engages with AI tools daily for data reconciliation and anomaly detection.
Uses AI for generating initial drafts of financial statements.
Relies on AI features for compliance checks and regulatory reporting.
Applies AI for expense and revenue forecasting on a weekly basis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI successfully flagged a recurring data entry error that had gone unnoticed.
An AI-generated report once misclassified several transactions, requiring manual correction.
Learned to always review AI-suggested adjustments before finalizing reports.
Found that AI accelerates routine tasks but still needs human oversight for complex cases.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Organization provides introductory webinars on new AI tools.
Peer-to-peer coaching is common for sharing AI tips and workflows.
There is limited formal training, making self-study and experimentation important.
Internal documentation covers basic troubleshooting for AI-powered features.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex tasks.
Often begins tasks independently, then checks or augments work with AI suggestions.
Values the ability to intervene and adjust AI-generated outputs as needed.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant that helps with routine and data-heavy tasks.
Leverages AI as an advisor for compliance checks and data-driven insights.
Treats AI as a co-worker for automating repetitive processes but retains final decision authority.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with new AI workflows that increase efficiency.
Willing to adapt processes if AI tools demonstrate clear value.
Requires some initial demonstration of reliability before fully integrating AI-driven workflows.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational AI interfaces more intuitive for complex queries.
Prefers command-based interfaces for batch processing or specific data retrieval.
Uses both styles depending on the task context and urgency.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Financial reporting and compliance often require nuanced understanding of regulations.
Judgment-based tasks, such as interpreting ambiguous data, depend on domain expertise.
AI is most trusted with standardized tasks rather than those needing contextual knowledge.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for initial data reconciliation and anomaly detection.
Always reviews AI-generated financial statements before submission.
Has moderate trust in AI for compliance checks, but verifies critical outputs.
Less likely to rely on AI for final decisions in high-stakes or ambiguous situations.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive and data-heavy processes.
Favors human review for tasks involving interpretation or regulatory compliance.
Sees optimal workflow as AI handling 60-70% of routine work, with humans reviewing and finalizing.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable when outputs are verifiable and tasks are low-risk.
Feels threatened by automation replacing judgment-intensive roles.
Supports automation as long as override and audit trails are available.
Accepts automation for standardized compliance tasks but prefers manual control for exceptions.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the need for analytical and oversight skills.
Routine data entry and reconciliation skills are becoming less critical.
Interpreting complex financial data remains a valuable human skill.
Adaptability and tech proficiency are increasingly important.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to audit and validate AI outputs is a new skill area.
Understanding AI decision logic and tuning parameters is becoming relevant.
Needs competency in integrating AI tools with existing accounting systems.
Responsible for staying updated on AI-driven compliance changes.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about AI automating repetitive accounting tasks.
Believes that oversight and interpretation roles are less likely to be replaced.
Sees opportunity for upskilling to remain relevant as AI adoption grows.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review of AI-generated reports is standard practice.
ERP systems allow for human overrides of AI-suggested adjustments.
Regular audits ensure that automated processes remain accurate.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has formalized guidelines for integrating AI into workflows.
Recent policy updates emphasize human review of AI outputs.
Team is encouraged to suggest improvements for AI tool implementation.
There is an ongoing review process for AI effectiveness and fairness.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Views AI as a major driver of efficiency and accuracy in finance.
Believes AI will transform routine accounting processes industry-wide.
Recognizes the potential for AI to reduce human error and increase transparency.
Cautious about unintended consequences, such as job displacement.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes transparency in AI decision-making is essential.
Worries about potential bias in AI-driven compliance checks.
Values regular audits and clear accountability for AI outputs.
Supports ethical guidelines for responsible AI adoption.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires seamless integration between AI tools and legacy accounting software.
Would benefit from AI-powered real-time compliance monitoring.
Seeks more transparent AI logic explanations for audit purposes.
Wishes for AI features that simplify regulatory updates.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine tasks to be almost fully automated by AI.
Foresees a shift toward oversight, analysis, and exception management.
Predicts greater emphasis on interpreting AI outputs and addressing edge cases.
Anticipates more collaboration with data scientists and AI specialists.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in ongoing training for staff on AI tools and best practices.
Establish clear policies for human oversight of AI-driven processes.
Encourage cross-functional collaboration between finance and IT for AI integration.
Regularly review and update AI tools to ensure compliance and transparency.
Foster a culture of ethical AI use and open feedback on automation impacts.
